Home
Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Analytic Philosophy of Mind

David Lewis

1941 – 2001

Analytic functionalism in a systematic metaphysics: folk psychology, qualia, and modal realism.

David Lewis’s philosophy of mind is best understood as a realizer-functional identity theory embedded in a much larger metaphysical project: Humean supervenience, reductive materialism, natural properties, possible worlds, decision theory, and a theory of interpretation. He is not merely “a functionalist” in the broad Putnam-Fodor sense, nor merely an old-style mind-brain identity theorist. His distinctive contribution is to combine three ideas that are often pulled apart: first, mental states are defined by their place in a causal-psychological theory; second, the states that actually occupy those roles are physical states; third, the totality of mental, semantic, and psychological truth is fixed by the physical arrangement of the world plus the interpretive constraints that make agents intelligible.

A compact formula for the Lewisian view is this:

Mental states are the actual occupants, in a suitable population and context, of the causal roles specified by folk psychology, with those occupants ultimately located in the physical world.

That sentence already contains most of the system. It says that Lewis is a realist about the mental; he thinks pain, belief, desire, intention, perception, and experience are real. It says that he is a materialist; the occupants of the relevant roles are physical or neural states, at least for ordinary human beings. It says that he is a functionalist in method; mental-state concepts are fixed by causal roles. It says that he is an identity theorist in ontology; the mental state is not an abstract role over and above its realizer, but the real state that realizes the role. It says that he is holist; individual mental states are identified through their place in an interconnected psychological theory. And it says that he is historically positioned at the point where behaviorism, identity theory, functionalism, and analytic metaphysics converge.

Lewis himself described his position as both realist and reductive materialist: mental states are not fictions or mere behavioral dispositions, but they are contingently identical with physical or neural states, in the spirit of the Australian materialist tradition of U. T. Place, J. J. C. Smart, and D. M. Armstrong. In the same retrospective discussion, he connects this to the claim that the whole truth about the world, including the mental truth, supervenes on the distribution of fundamental physical properties.

1. Historical location: from behaviorism and identity theory to functionalism

Lewis enters philosophy of mind after the collapse of two earlier programs: logical behaviorism and simple identity theory.

Logical behaviorism attempted to analyze mental-state talk in terms of actual or possible behavior. To say that someone is in pain, for example, would be to say something about tendencies to wince, groan, avoid injury, say “that hurts,” and so on. The attraction was anti-Cartesian clarity: no mysterious inner episodes. But the view struggled because mental states are not tied directly to behavior in any simple way. Pain does not always produce groaning; belief does not always produce assertion; desire does not always produce pursuit. Behavior depends on other mental states. A person in pain may remain silent because she wants to appear brave. A person who believes it is raining may not take an umbrella because she wants to get wet. Mental states are therefore not analyzable one by one into behavioral dispositions unless those dispositions are themselves relativized to other beliefs, desires, perceptions, intentions, and background conditions.

The mind-brain identity theory arose in the 1950s, especially in the work of Place, Feigl, and Smart. According to this theory, mental states and processes are identical with brain states and processes; the theory denies irreducible non-physical qualia, while also distinguishing ontological identity from any claim that mental vocabulary is simply synonymous with neurological vocabulary. The identity theorists’ central analogy was scientific identification: lightning is electrical discharge, heat is molecular motion, water is H₂O, and pain might be C-fiber firing or some other neural process. The problem was that simple type-identity looked too restrictive. Could non-human animals, Martians, or machines feel pain if their internal physical realizers differed from ours? If pain is strictly identical with a particular human neural state-type, then creatures with different hardware seem excluded by definition.

Functionalism developed as an answer to this problem. Functionalism defines mental states not by their physical constitution, nor by their outward behavioral expression alone, but by their causal relations to inputs, outputs, and other mental states. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy characterizes functionalism as the doctrine that what makes something a mental state is not what it is made of, but the role it plays in the cognitive life of the organism. That role includes typical causes, typical effects, and relations to other mental states. Pain, on this view, is the state typically caused by bodily damage, tending to cause aversive behavior, attention, distress, desire for relief, and further beliefs about injury.

Early functionalism had several variants. Turing and Putnam inspired machine-state functionalism, where mental states are modeled on computational states realized by different hardware. Later philosophers developed analytic functionalism, which analyzes mental concepts through ordinary psychological platitudes, and psychofunctionalism, which treats mental states as the states postulated by mature empirical cognitive psychology.

Lewis’s position is located precisely at the intersection of these developments. He accepts the functionalist insight that mental states are identified by causal role. But he also accepts the identity theorist’s ontological conclusion that, once we discover what physical states occupy those roles, the mental states are identical with those physical states. In this respect, Lewis is not a pure role functionalist, if that means that pain is an abstract functional property multiply realized by different physical states. He is closer to what later came to be called realizer functionalism: pain, for a given kind of creature, is the state that realizes the pain-role in that kind of creature. The SEP explicitly treats Lewis’s 1972 work as the source of the Ramsey-sentence method for defining mental states and distinguishes his analytic functionalism from psychofunctionalism.

This is why Lewis is historically important. He gave identity theory a functionalist semantics. He gave functionalism a materialist ontology. And he gave philosophy of mind a general method for connecting ordinary psychological discourse with scientific reduction.

2. The relevant Lewisian corpus

Lewis’s philosophy of mind is scattered across papers rather than contained in one systematic monograph. The most important texts include:

“An Argument for the Identity Theory” from 1966, where Lewis first gives a functional-causal argument for identifying experiences with physical states.

“How to Define Theoretical Terms” from 1970, where he develops the general method for defining theoretical terms by their place in a theory. The paper’s abstract describes theoretical terms as implicitly defined by the theories in which they occur, with their meanings characterized by causal roles; this became influential for functionalism and reduction.

“Psychophysical and Theoretical Identifications” from 1972, where Lewis applies the method of theoretical terms to mental states and psychophysical identity.

“Radical Interpretation” from 1974, where Lewis develops his theory of how beliefs, desires, meanings, and rationality are simultaneously interpreted.

“Survival and Identity” from 1976, where he treats persons as aggregates of person-stages related in the right ways, connecting philosophy of mind with personal identity.

“Attitudes De Dicto and De Se” from 1979, where he argues that attitudes are fundamentally relations to properties rather than only to propositions. Oxford’s abstract summarizes the central thesis: attitudes are relations to properties, and the de se subsumes the de dicto.

“Mad Pain and Martian Pain” from 1980, where he refines his functional identity theory to handle abnormal humans and non-human realizers.

“What Experience Teaches” from 1988, where he responds to the knowledge argument by defending an ability account of knowing what an experience is like.

“Desire as Belief” and “Desire as Belief II,” where he discusses motivation, Humeanism, and decision theory. The first paper’s abstract frames the issue as whether rejecting Humean motivation requires rejecting decision theory.

“Reduction of Mind” from 1994, where Lewis retrospectively places his philosophy of mind inside his broader reductive materialism.

“Naming the Colours” from 1997, where his philosophy of mind connects with color, perception, naming, and epistemology.

Taken together, these works yield not just a theory of pain or belief but a full philosophy of psychology: a theory of mental-state concepts, mental ontology, psychological explanation, phenomenal consciousness, intentional content, rational agency, and personal identity.

3. The metaphysical background: Humean supervenience and reductive materialism

Lewis’s philosophy of mind cannot be separated from his metaphysics. His mature system is organized by the doctrine he calls Humean supervenience. In broad terms, Humean supervenience says that all truths about the world supervene on the distribution of perfectly natural local properties and relations across spacetime. The mental, semantic, modal, causal, moral, and nomological facts are not additional fundamental furniture of reality. They are fixed, in one way or another, by the base-level arrangement of the world.

The SEP’s entry on Lewis states that, for Lewis, the world is fundamentally a distribution of perfectly natural properties and relations, and that mental facts, contents, values, laws, and chances must be located within that Humean mosaic. The point is not that psychology is dispensable. It is that psychology is not metaphysically fundamental. Psychological truths are genuine, but they are higher-level truths whose obtaining is determined by lower-level facts.

This matters for philosophy of mind in several ways.

First, Lewis has no room for Cartesian mental substances. Persons, minds, experiences, and intentional states must all be located within the physical world.

Second, he has no room for brute psychophysical laws connecting nonphysical qualia to physical states. If pain is real, then pain must be part of the physical world, not an extra nonphysical accompaniment.

Third, mental causation is not a special metaphysical problem for him in the way it becomes for some nonreductive physicalists. If a mental state is identical with a physical state, then its causal efficacy is the causal efficacy of that physical state. My desire for water causes me to walk to the kitchen because that desire is a physical-psychological state that participates in the causal order.

Fourth, psychological explanation remains legitimate. Reduction does not mean elimination. A storm is physical, but meteorology is not replaced by particle physics in ordinary explanatory practice. Likewise, belief, desire, and pain are physically realized, but psychological explanation can still be indispensable, higher-level, and lawlike enough for human purposes.

Lewis’s metaphysical project is also methodologically holistic. His metaphysics is not an isolated doctrine but a regulatory framework for his work in language, mind, modality, causation, and value; the SEP describes Lewis’s work as guided by a kind of systematic reflective equilibrium in which metaphysics regulates the rest of philosophy. This is crucial. Lewis’s philosophy of mind is not a local theory designed only to solve the mind-body problem. It is one part of a larger attempt to explain how all apparently nonfundamental phenomena can be real without being fundamental.

4. The Ramsey-Lewis method: mental terms as theoretical terms

Lewis’s most important technical contribution to philosophy of mind is his use of the Ramsey-Carnap-Lewis method for defining theoretical terms.

The basic idea is this. A scientific or commonsense theory introduces terms whose reference is fixed by the role those terms play in the theory. “Electron,” “gene,” “temperature,” and “pain” are not defined by direct acquaintance with an object. They are introduced by a network of claims. The theoretical term denotes whatever entity, property, or state uniquely or best satisfies the relevant theoretical role.

Lewis applies this to folk psychology. Folk psychology is the commonsense theory that relates perception, belief, desire, intention, emotion, memory, pain, action, and behavior. The SEP notes that Lewis treated everyday psychology as a term-introducing theory and used the method of theoretical terms to derive a functionalist account.

The formal structure is as follows.

Suppose a psychological theory T contains mental terms M₁, M₂, M₃, and so on. T says things such as:

Pain is typically caused by bodily damage.

Pain typically causes distress, attention to the damaged body part, desire for relief, and avoidance behavior.

Beliefs are typically caused by perception and inference.

Desires and beliefs jointly cause intentional action.

Memory connects past perception with later belief.

Fear is caused by beliefs about danger and desires to avoid harm.

The Ramsey method replaces the mental terms with variables:

There exist x₁, x₂, x₃, … such that x₁ is typically caused by bodily damage, x₁ causes distress and avoidance, x₂ is caused by perception, x₃ combines with x₂ to produce action, and so on.

Then the mental terms are defined as the occupants of those roles. Pain is the x that plays the pain-role. Belief is the y that plays the belief-role. Desire is the z that plays the desire-role. The terms are not defined in isolation; they are defined simultaneously by the whole theory.

This gives Lewis a way to avoid both behaviorism and introspectionism. Pain is not defined merely by outward behavior, because its role includes relations to other mental states. But pain is not an ineffable private object either, because its identity is fixed by its causal-theoretical role.

The SEP’s entry on Lewis emphasizes that this method introduces mental states through “folk psychology” as a term-introducing theory and then identifies the mental states with the realizers of the corresponding roles, subject to complications when the theory has no unique realizer or has multiple imperfect realizers.

This is the engine of Lewis’s philosophy of mind.

5. The identity argument: from causal role to physical realizer

Lewis’s argument for mind-brain identity can be reconstructed as follows.

First, mental states are defined by their causal roles in folk psychology. Pain, for instance, is the state that occupies the pain-role: the state typically caused by bodily damage and typically causing distress, aversion, desire for relief, avoidance, reports of pain, and related mental effects.

Second, empirical neuroscience discovers that a certain physical or neural state occupies that role in human beings. Let that state be N.

Third, if pain is the occupant of the pain-role, and N is the occupant of the pain-role, then pain is N.

So the identity statement is not a speculative metaphysical guess. It is the conclusion of a two-stage procedure: conceptual role-fixing followed by empirical discovery. The conceptual part tells us what kind of state pain must be. The scientific part tells us which state actually fits the description.

The SEP’s entry on Lewis reconstructs this argument directly: pain is defined by its causal role; the physical closure of the world implies that some neural state occupies that role; therefore, pain is that neural state. The SEP’s entry on identity theory also says that Lewis’s work was valuable because it married identity theory with functionalist ideas, using Ramseyfication to make folk psychology compatible with materialism.

This is the decisive difference between Lewis and a role functionalist. A role functionalist might say that pain is the second-order property of having some state or other that plays the pain-role. Lewis’s identity-theoretic tendency is to say that pain is the first-order state that actually plays the role, at least relative to a relevant population. For humans, pain may be one neural state-type; for Martians, another; for some other species, something else. There is no one universal physical state that pain must be across all possible creatures. But there can still be genuine identities within kinds.

This view is sometimes called local type identity theory. It preserves the identity theorist’s claim that mental states are physical states, while avoiding the chauvinism of saying that every possible pain must be the same human neural state.

6. Contingent identity and the role-realizer distinction

Lewis’s psychophysical identities are often called contingent, but this must be handled carefully.

In Kripkean modal semantics, if “pain” and “N” are rigid designators and pain is identical with N, then the identity is necessary if true. Kripke used this kind of reasoning to challenge identity theory. Later critics, including Chalmers, developed modal and conceivability arguments against reductive physicalism; the SEP notes that Kripkean and Chalmers-style objections made consciousness a central difficulty for materialist identity theories.

Lewis’s response is not to deny logic. Rather, he treats many mental-state terms as theoretical terms whose reference is fixed by role descriptions. Role descriptions need not designate the same state in every possible world. “The occupant of the pain-role” may pick out one neural state in humans, another state in Martians, and perhaps no state in a dysfunctional case. The role is fixed conceptually; the realizer is discovered empirically; the realizer can vary by kind, population, or world.

This is why the role-realizer distinction is crucial.

The role is the functional-causal specification supplied by folk psychology.

The realizer is the state that actually plays that role in a given kind of system.

The mental state, for Lewis, is generally the realizer rather than the role considered abstractly. That is why he can be both a functionalist about the meaning or analysis of mental concepts and an identity theorist about the ontology of mental states.

7. “Mad Pain and Martian Pain”: the key refinement

Lewis’s “Mad Pain and Martian Pain” is the central text for understanding the sophistication of his view.

The two cases are designed to expose opposite pressures.

Mad pain is pain in a human whose causal relations are abnormal. Suppose someone has a state that is caused not by damage but by exercise, or that causes not aversion but mathematical calculation, or that leads the subject to seek rather than avoid injury. We still want to say, at least in some cases, that the person is in pain. But if pain is defined strictly by its usual causal role, abnormal pain seems impossible.

Martian pain is pain in a creature whose internal physical constitution differs radically from ours. Martians may have hydraulic, silicon, or alien biological states occupying the pain-role. We still want to allow that they have pain. But if pain is identical with a human neural state, Martian pain seems impossible.

Lewis’s solution is kind-relative. Pain is not defined by what plays the pain-role in one individual at one moment. It is defined by what plays the pain-role for the relevant population or kind. For ordinary humans, pain is the state that typically occupies the pain-role in humans. The madman can have pain because he has the human pain-state, even if that state produces abnormal effects in him. The Martian can have pain because a different state occupies the pain-role in Martians. The SEP summarizes Lewis’s response in just these terms: pains are defined relative to kinds; humans and Martians can have different state-types as their pains, while mad humans can still share the human pain-state despite abnormal causal patterns.

This is one of Lewis’s most elegant moves. It avoids three unsatisfactory extremes.

It avoids behaviorism, because the madman can be in pain even when his behavior is abnormal.

It avoids human chauvinism, because Martians can be in pain despite different internal realizers.

It avoids excessive liberalism, because not just any state counts as pain; it must occupy the right role in the right kind of system.

But the solution also reveals a deep feature of Lewis’s theory: mental-state attribution is not purely individualistic. It depends partly on the kind to which the individual belongs. What state counts as pain in a creature may depend on the normal psychological organization of that creature’s population. The SEP notes that Lewis’s account has an anti-individualistic element of this kind, because contents and mental states may be determined relative to a subject’s kind or population.

8. Folk psychology as theory: the foundation and its vulnerability

Lewis’s philosophy of mind depends heavily on the idea that folk psychology is a theory. It is not merely a collection of words. It is a structured network of tacit generalizations about how perception, belief, desire, sensation, intention, memory, and action interact.

The folk theory says, roughly, that perception tends to produce belief; belief and desire tend to produce action; pain tends to be caused by injury and tends to produce aversion; hunger tends to produce desire for food; memory preserves information from earlier experience; emotion affects attention, desire, and action; and so on. Mental terms get their reference by occupying nodes in this network.

This makes Lewis an analytic functionalist: the relevant roles are supplied by commonsense psychological platitudes. The SEP describes analytic functionalism as defining mental states through commonsense platitudes about the causal roles of mental states, while psychofunctionalism instead looks to mature empirical psychology.

But Lewis’s use of folk psychology is not crude. He does not require every folk platitude to be true. A theory can be approximately true, and its terms can refer if there are entities that sufficiently realize its central claims. Scientific terms often survive revisions of theory. “Atom” did not become meaningless when ancient atomism was revised. Likewise, “belief” or “pain” need not collapse because some folk generalizations are false.

Still, this is a major vulnerability. The SEP’s entry on folk psychology notes several criticisms of the Lewisian approach: the semantic theory of theoretical terms was challenged by Kaplan, Donnellan, Putnam, and Kripke; it is uncertain whether folk psychology is empirically successful; Lewis did not actually survey the full range of folk platitudes; folk conceptions may include noncausal elements; and there are questions about whether folk psychology is coherent enough to define mental terms.

This objection matters because the entire Lewisian reduction begins with the role-fixing theory. If folk psychology is too vague, false, culturally variable, or internally inconsistent, then mental terms may lack determinate reference. Lewis can reply that reference only requires enough structure and enough approximate realization. But the reply comes at a cost: the more flexible the theory becomes, the less sharp the reduction appears.

9. Scientific psychology and the fate of folk psychology

Lewis’s view is often associated with folk psychology, but it also has implications for scientific psychology.

On a Lewisian account, ordinary mental terms are introduced by folk theory, but empirical psychology and neuroscience discover the actual realizers of the roles. Scientific psychology may also correct folk psychology. For example, folk psychology may treat memory as a simple storage system, whereas cognitive science reveals multiple memory systems, constructive recall, implicit memory, and so forth. A Lewisian can say that the original term “memory” refers to whatever states or systems best occupy the folk role, but scientific refinement may split that role into more precise kinds.

This gives Lewis a moderate position between eliminativism and uncritical common sense.

He is not an eliminativist in the style of Paul Churchland. He does not think ordinary mental states are obviously fictional theoretical posits awaiting replacement. He thinks folk psychology is successful enough to define real states.

But he is not committed to every folk-psychological category as scientifically fundamental. “Belief,” “desire,” “pain,” “intention,” and “memory” may be coarse-grained, multiply realized, or scientifically decomposable. Their legitimacy depends on their explanatory success and on whether there are real states that occupy the relevant roles.

This is an attractive feature of Lewis’s theory. It respects ordinary psychological explanation while leaving room for empirical correction. The reduction is not supposed to be armchair neuroscience. It is a two-level project: philosophy clarifies the role; science identifies the realizer.

10. Intentionality: belief, desire, content, and interpretation

Lewis’s theory of mind is not only a theory of sensations such as pain. It is also a theory of intentional states: belief, desire, intention, expectation, preference, fear, hope, and other attitudes directed toward contents.

Here Lewis’s work connects with “Radical Interpretation,” decision theory, convention, language, and natural properties. The basic thought is that beliefs and desires are assigned to agents by interpreting them as rational enough, coherent enough, and responsive enough to the world. Interpretation is holistic. We cannot assign beliefs independently of desires, or desires independently of beliefs, or meanings independently of both. We interpret the whole system together.

Lewis differs from Donald Davidson in important ways. The SEP’s entry on Lewis emphasizes that, unlike Davidson, Lewis does not make language primary, does not require beliefs to be mostly true in the same way, allows narrow content, and treats mental content as primary. For Lewis, interpretation is governed by fit and rationalization: assigned contents should make sense of the subject’s behavior, dispositions, perceptions, and preferences.

But fit alone underdetermines interpretation. Many assignments of belief and desire can rationalize the same behavior. Here Lewis’s metaphysics of natural properties enters. Naturalness helps select the objectively better interpretation. If one interpretation attributes beliefs and desires involving more natural properties, and another uses bizarre gerrymandered properties, the former is better. This is Lewis’s answer to problems of indeterminacy such as grue-like predicates, quus-like meanings, and gavagai-style translation ambiguity. The SEP notes that naturalness becomes central in Lewis’s later account of content, rationality, language, and laws, helping solve underdetermination problems.

So Lewis’s theory of intentionality has three layers.

At the first layer, belief and desire are functional states within folk psychology: beliefs are states caused by perception and inference and combined with desires to produce action; desires are states that represent goals or preferred outcomes and combine with beliefs to guide behavior.

At the second layer, contents are assigned by interpretation: we attribute the beliefs and desires that best rationalize the agent’s behavior and psychological organization.

At the third layer, naturalness constrains interpretation: among competing assignments, those involving more natural properties are metaphysically and semantically privileged.

This gives Lewis a powerful but controversial account. It avoids treating intentionality as a primitive mental relation. But it relies on the metaphysics of naturalness to prevent radical indeterminacy.

11. Desire, motivation, and Humean psychology

Lewis is broadly Humean about motivation. Beliefs represent how the world is; desires represent how the agent wants the world to be; action is explained by the interaction of belief and desire. If I believe there is water in the kitchen and desire water, that combination helps explain why I walk to the kitchen.

In “Desire as Belief,” Lewis discusses an anti-Humean proposal that desire can be reduced to a kind of belief, especially belief about what is good or what one ought to do. The abstract of the paper frames Lewis’s argument as a defense of the Humean theory of motivation and claims that rejecting it would require rejecting decision theory.

This matters for Lewis’s philosophy of mind because his psychology is not merely causal-functional; it is also decision-theoretic. Beliefs and desires are the states that make agents interpretable as choosing under uncertainty. Decision theory gives structure to the attribution of credences, preferences, utilities, and rational action. Lewis’s philosophy of psychology is therefore not just a theory of mental ontology; it is also a theory of rational agency.

But Lewis does not reduce psychology to ideal rationality. Real agents are imperfect, inconsistent, inattentive, and irrational. Interpretation must balance charity with empirical fit. The agent must be rational enough to be interpretable, but not perfectly rational. Lewis’s view is thus more flexible than a strict ideal-agent theory.

12. De dicto and de se: the self-locating mind

One of Lewis’s most important contributions to philosophy of mind is his account of de se attitudes.

Traditional philosophy often models belief as a relation between a subject and a proposition. To believe that snow is white is to stand in the belief relation to the proposition that snow is white. Lewis argues that this model is insufficient. Some beliefs are essentially self-locating. They concern not merely which possible world is actual, but where one is within the world.

Classic examples include the amnesiac lost in a library, the person who sees a trail of sugar and then realizes “I am making the mess,” or two people who know all objective facts but do not know which person they themselves are. The SEP’s entry on self-locating belief explains that Lewis and Perry developed influential proposals using cases such as Lingens in the Stanford library and Perry’s messy shopper.

Lewis’s solution is that attitudes are relations not primarily to propositions but to properties. To believe de se is to self-ascribe a property. If I believe “I am in danger,” I self-ascribe the property of being in danger. If I believe “I am David Lewis,” I self-ascribe the property of being David Lewis. A centered-world formulation treats content as involving a world, a time, and an individual; the SEP explains that Lewis models self-locating content using centered worlds and properties related to individuals at worlds.

This has major consequences.

First, it makes de se belief fundamental to action. Purely impersonal propositions do not explain action unless the agent locates herself within them. Knowing that David Lewis is in danger will not cause me to duck unless I also know that I am David Lewis, or that I am the person in danger.

Second, it extends Lewis’s functionalism into intentional content. Beliefs are not just internal states with causal roles; they are states whose contents are fixed by how agents locate themselves in the space of possibilities.

Third, it supports a property-based theory of all attitudes. De dicto belief becomes a special case: to believe that P is to self-ascribe the property of inhabiting a world where P. This is why the Oxford abstract says that, for Lewis, the de se subsumes the de dicto.

Fourth, it connects philosophy of mind with metaphysics of possible worlds. Lewis’s modal realism is not a decorative background doctrine here. His theory of content uses possible individuals, centered worlds, and properties across worlds as tools for analyzing psychological attitudes.

13. Consciousness, qualia, and “what experience teaches”

Lewis’s most difficult topic is phenomenal consciousness. His theory handles pain as a causal-functional state, but consciousness raises the familiar question: can causal role and physical realization fully capture what experience is like?

Lewis’s answer is reductive. He does not deny experience. He denies that experience introduces nonphysical facts. His best-known treatment is “What Experience Teaches,” where he responds to the knowledge argument associated with Frank Jackson’s Mary case.

The knowledge argument says: Mary knows all physical facts about color vision while confined to a black-and-white room. When she first sees red, she learns what red experience is like. Therefore, there is some fact about experience she did not know before. Therefore, not all facts are physical facts. The SEP summarizes the knowledge argument as an argument that complete physical knowledge still leaves out knowledge of nonphysical phenomenal properties.

Lewis replies with the ability hypothesis. When Mary leaves the room, she does not learn a new fact. She gains new abilities: the ability to recognize red experiences, remember them, imagine them, and perhaps predict certain behavior or reactions. The abstract of “What Experience Teaches” summarizes Lewis’s position: knowing what an experience is like is not possession of new phenomenal information, but possession of abilities to remember, imagine, and recognize. The SEP’s entry on the knowledge argument identifies Lewis and Nemirow as central defenders of this ability response and notes Lewis’s emphasis on recognition, imagination, and behavioral prediction.

This response fits perfectly into Lewis’s system. Experience is physical-functional. What Mary lacked was not a fact outside physics but practical acquaintance or ability generated by undergoing the experience. Lewis can therefore preserve reductive materialism while explaining why Mary’s discovery feels epistemically significant.

But this is also one of the most contested parts of his philosophy of mind. Critics argue that Mary seems to acquire knowledge-that, not merely know-how. She seems to learn that this is what red experience is like. Others argue that recognitional and imaginative abilities are neither necessary nor sufficient for phenomenal knowledge. The SEP summarizes objections to the ability hypothesis along these lines, including the worry that know-how itself may be a kind of knowledge-that.

The broader challenge is the explanatory gap. Functional and physical accounts may identify the causal role of pain or color experience, but critics ask why that physical-functional state feels the way it does. The SEP’s functionalism entry notes that zombies, explanatory-gap arguments, and phenomenal-concept strategies became central objections and responses in debates over functionalism and consciousness. Lewis’s own answer is uncompromisingly physicalist: once the role is fixed and the realizer is identified, there is no further nonphysical fact left to explain. Whether that is satisfying is the central dispute.

14. Perception and color

Lewis’s work on color is less central than his work on pain, belief, and de se attitudes, but it matters because color experience is one of the main test cases for theories of mind. In “Naming the Colours,” he addresses color, naming, perception, and epistemology; PhilPapers classifies the paper under color terms in philosophy of mind and lists keywords including color, epistemology, naming, and perception.

A Lewisian treatment of color must coordinate several commitments.

Color experience is a real mental phenomenon.

Color terms and color concepts are fixed partly by perceptual and linguistic practices.

Color experiences are physically realized states of perceivers.

Color properties, if genuine, must fit within the natural-property structure of the world or be reducible to response-dependent or dispositional patterns.

Color knowledge, like Mary’s knowledge after seeing red, does not require irreducible phenomenal facts.

Thus color is not a marginal issue. It tests the relation between perception, language, natural properties, and phenomenal consciousness. Lewis’s treatment is consistent with his broader pattern: preserve ordinary discourse where possible, analyze its role, then locate its truthmakers in the physical world.

15. Personal identity and the psychological person

Lewis’s philosophy of mind also touches personal identity. In “Survival and Identity,” he treats persons not as Cartesian egos but as suitably related aggregates of person-stages. The bibliographic abstract describes persons as aggregates of person-stages related in the right way.

This view fits his broader metaphysics. Lewis is a perdurantist: persisting things have temporal parts. A person is not an indivisible soul enduring wholly through time, but a temporally extended entity composed of stages. Psychological continuity and connectedness help explain survival, memory, anticipation, prudential concern, and self-regard.

This has implications for philosophy of mind. The subject of experience is not a simple mental substance. It is a physical-temporal continuant organized by psychological relations. De se attitudes are then attitudes of person-stages that locate themselves within a world and within a temporal-personal sequence. Memory, anticipation, and identity are not primitive mental relations; they are functional-psychological and metaphysical relations among stages.

The result is a thoroughly anti-Cartesian theory of the person. Mental unity is real, but it is constituted by relations among physical and psychological stages, not by an immaterial ego.

16. The architecture of a comprehensive Lewisian philosophy of mind

From these materials, we can extract a comprehensive Lewisian philosophy of mind as a set of systematic theses.

First, ontological realism about the mental. Mental states are real. Pain, belief, desire, intention, perception, and experience are not mere fictions or linguistic conveniences.

Second, reductive materialism. Mental states are not fundamental nonphysical entities. They are located in the physical world and ultimately supervene on the distribution of physical properties. Lewis explicitly presents himself as a realist and reductive materialist about mind.

Third, functional role-fixing. Mental concepts are fixed by causal roles within folk psychology. To know what pain is, conceptually, is to know its place in the network of causes and effects involving injury, distress, aversion, belief, desire, and behavior.

Fourth, Ramseyan holism. Mental terms are defined together, not one by one. Belief, desire, pain, perception, memory, and intention form an interconnected theoretical structure. The Ramsey-Lewis method replaces mental terms with variables and identifies them with the things that satisfy the theory.

Fifth, realizer identity. The mental state is the real state that occupies the functional role, not merely the abstract role itself. For humans, mental states are neural or physical states; for other creatures, they may be different physical states.

Sixth, kind-relative multiple realization. Pain in humans and pain in Martians need not be the same physical state-type. But each can be the state that occupies the pain-role for its own kind. This solves the Mad Pain and Martian Pain problem while preserving identity theory.

Seventh, psychological explanation without metaphysical autonomy. Psychology is explanatorily legitimate, but psychological truths are not fundamental additions to the physical facts.

Eighth, belief-desire psychology and Humean motivation. Action is explained by the interaction of belief and desire. Desire is not reducible to belief without threatening the structure of decision theory.

Ninth, interpretivism about content. Belief and desire contents are assigned by the best interpretation of agents as rational, coherent, and responsive to the world.

Tenth, naturalness as a constraint on content. Because behavior underdetermines interpretation, more natural properties help determine which contents agents really have.

Eleventh, de se priority. Belief is fundamentally self-ascription of properties. The de dicto is a special case of the de se.

Twelfth, physicalism about consciousness. Phenomenal experience is real but not nonphysical. Knowing what an experience is like is a matter of acquiring abilities, not learning new nonphysical facts.

Thirteenth, anti-Cartesian personal identity. Persons are material-temporal beings organized by psychological continuity, not immaterial souls.

Fourteenth, systematic supervenience. All mental, psychological, semantic, and personal facts supervene on the physical-natural arrangement of the world, together with the interpretive and theoretical structures that make those facts higher-level facts.

17. Lewis’s place in the larger development of philosophy of mind

Lewis belongs to the second major generation of analytic philosophy of mind.

The first generation was dominated by behaviorism and early identity theory. Behaviorism sought public criteria and rejected inner mental objects. Identity theory restored inner states but identified them directly with brain states.

The second generation developed functionalism. Functionalism allowed mental states to be internal, causally efficacious, and multiply realizable. Lewis is one of the decisive figures in this transition. He did not simply abandon identity theory for functionalism; he reconstructed identity theory through functionalism.

In this respect, his position differs from Hilary Putnam’s early functionalism. Putnam emphasized multiple realizability against type identity. Lewis accepts multiple realizability but uses it to produce local identities. The result is not “pain is one functional role over and above all its realizers,” but “pain, in a given kind, is the realizer of the pain-role for that kind.”

His position also differs from Fodor-style computational functionalism. Lewis is not primarily giving a computational architecture of cognition. He is giving a semantic-metaphysical account of mental-state terms and their reduction.

His position differs from Davidson’s anomalous monism. Davidson denies strict psychophysical laws and emphasizes interpretation, rationality, and anomalism. Lewis also emphasizes interpretation, but he is more reductive, more naturalistic, more comfortable with psychophysical identification, and more willing to use natural properties as objective constraints.

His position differs from eliminative materialism. Lewis thinks folk psychology is sufficiently successful to define real states. He does not think “belief” and “desire” are obviously like “phlogiston.”

His position differs from nonreductive physicalism. Lewis does not want mental properties to float as autonomous higher-level properties with their own irreducible causal powers. He wants identities, realizers, and supervenience.

His position differs from dualism and property dualism. He denies that phenomenal consciousness requires irreducible nonphysical properties.

This makes Lewis a central bridge figure. He connects the old materialist identity theory to analytic functionalism, and he connects both to late twentieth-century metaphysics of supervenience, possible worlds, natural properties, and reduction.

18. Major strengths of the Lewisian view

The first strength is systematic unity. Lewis gives one integrated treatment of mental ontology, mental semantics, psychological explanation, intentional content, phenomenal consciousness, and personal identity. Many philosophers solve one problem by worsening another. Lewis’s system is designed to make all the parts fit.

The second strength is its treatment of mental causation. If mental states are physical realizers of causal roles, then they are causally efficacious without overdetermination or exclusion. My pain causes me to withdraw because my pain is a physical state playing a causal role.

The third strength is its balance between common sense and science. Folk psychology fixes the roles; science discovers the realizers. This respects ordinary mental discourse while allowing empirical correction.

The fourth strength is its handling of multiple realization. Lewis avoids the crude identity theorist mistake of identifying all pain everywhere with one human neural state. But he also avoids a metaphysically inflated functionalism that treats mental states as detached second-order properties. Kind-relative realizer identity gives him a middle path.

The fifth strength is the depth of his account of intentionality. By combining interpretation, rationality, decision theory, naturalness, and de se content, Lewis gives a richer psychology than a simple causal-role theory alone would provide.

The sixth strength is metaphysical economy. Lewis does not multiply entities. No Cartesian souls, no primitive phenomenal properties, no brute semantic relations, no fundamental intentionality. The system is austere but not eliminative.

19. Major weaknesses and pressure points

The first weakness is dependence on folk psychology. If folk psychology is too indeterminate, false, or culturally variable, then the functional definitions become unstable. Lewis can appeal to approximate realization, but too much approximation threatens determinacy.

The second weakness is the status of consciousness. The ability hypothesis remains controversial. Many philosophers think Mary learns not merely how to recognize red experiences but what red experience is like as a fact. The SEP notes sustained objections to the ability hypothesis, including challenges to whether abilities are necessary or sufficient and whether know-how reduces to knowledge-that.

The third weakness is the explanatory gap. Even if neuroscience identifies the physical realizer of pain, critics argue that we still do not understand why that realizer has the phenomenal character it does. Lewis’s view is that no further fact remains. But that is exactly what opponents deny.

The fourth weakness is the metaphysical weight carried by naturalness. Lewis uses natural properties to solve underdetermination of content and interpretation. But if naturalness is obscure, primitive, or controversial, then the theory of intentionality inherits that obscurity.

The fifth weakness is kind-relativity. Mad Pain and Martian Pain are handled elegantly, but kind membership can be vague. What is the relevant kind: species, biological type, cognitive architecture, individual history, local population, or functional organization? Lewis’s account needs enough flexibility to handle cases, but enough constraint to avoid arbitrariness.

The sixth weakness is externalism. Putnam and Burge-style externalist arguments suggest that mental content may depend on environmental and social factors, not only internal causal-functional organization. The SEP’s functionalism entry notes that Twin Earth and Burge cases challenged individualistic functionalism by showing that content can depend on external linguistic and environmental relations. Lewis has resources here because he allows kind-relative and interpretive external constraints, but a purely internalist reading of his functionalism would be inadequate.

The seventh weakness is the relation between role and realizer. If pain is the realizer, then Martian pain and human pain are different states. What, then, makes them both pain? The answer is that they occupy analogous roles. But then the role seems to do the classificatory work after all. Lewis’s system must carefully distinguish the state that is pain in a given kind from the cross-kind concept under which those states fall.

20. Final synthesis: what is Lewis’s philosophy of mind?

The most accurate name for Lewis’s view is:

Ramsey-functional, kind-relative, realizer-identity, reductive materialism about mind.

Expanded, this means:

Mental discourse is theoretical discourse. Its terms are introduced by the commonsense theory of persons as perceivers, believers, desirers, feelers, rememberers, deliberators, and agents. The meanings or references of mental terms are fixed by the causal and rational roles they occupy in that theory. When the world contains states that occupy those roles, mental terms refer to those states. Empirical science then tells us what those states are. In humans, they are neural or physical states. In other creatures, they may be other physical states. Conscious experience does not escape this framework; what experience teaches is not a new nonphysical fact but a new set of recognitional and imaginative abilities. Intentional content is not primitive either; it is assigned through interpretation constrained by rationality, behavior, environment, and natural properties. Persons are not Cartesian subjects but temporally extended physical beings organized by psychological relations. All of this supervenes on the physical arrangement of the world.

Lewis’s philosophy of mind is therefore not a minor episode within functionalism. It is one of the most systematic attempts in late twentieth-century analytic philosophy to show how the mental can be real, causally powerful, semantically rich, phenomenologically serious, and yet wholly natural.

Its deepest ambition is reconciliation: common sense with science, functionalism with identity theory, mental realism with physicalism, and psychological explanation with metaphysical austerity. Its deepest vulnerability is that the phenomena most resistant to reduction — consciousness, intentional content, and first-person self-location — are precisely the phenomena it tries to domesticate within a general metaphysics of roles, realizers, interpretation, and supervenience. Whether one finds Lewis convincing depends largely on whether one thinks that once the causal, interpretive, and physical facts are fixed, the mental facts are thereby fixed too. Lewis’s answer is yes, and his philosophical system is perhaps the most rigorous version of that yes.

PreviousJerry Fodor NextThomas Nagel

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
